title: Reading Oscillating Loss Curves
concept: Oscillating (not converging, not diverging, just bouncing) GAN loss curves are common — and often a sign of healthy, ongoing competition rather than a failed training run.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why do GAN loss curves tend to bounce instead of smoothly flattening out?

options.text: Because each network is chasing a target that keeps changing as the other network updates
Because loss functions for GANs are calculated incorrectly
Because GPUs round loss values randomly

exercises.explain: Right — moving targets produce bouncing curves. Today: how to actually read those bounces.

cards.title: A flat line isn't always the good sign
cards.body: In most ML training, a flat, low loss curve means 'success.' But stare at a GAN's loss curves expecting that same flat line, and you might panic over something that's actually healthy — or miss something that's actually broken.

cards.title: Three shapes to tell apart
cards.body: GAN loss curves generally fall into three patterns: converging (settling toward a stable value — rare and not always meaningful for GANs), diverging (one or both losses shooting off toward infinity or zero — a real problem), and oscillating (bouncing within a range over time — often just normal back-and-forth competition).

cards.title: Reading it like a heart-rate monitor
cards.body: A healthy heartbeat on a monitor isn't a flat line — it's a steady, repeating up-and-down rhythm. A flat line means something is badly wrong. GAN loss curves are similar: gentle, regular bouncing within a reasonable range can be a sign the two networks are actively competing and both still learning, not a sign of failure.

cards.title: Normal bounce vs. real trouble
cards.body: Discriminator loss wobbling between 0.5 and 0.9 for hundreds of epochs while generated samples keep visibly improving? Probably normal oscillation. Discriminator loss suddenly rocketing toward 0 (or generator loss toward infinity) and samples turning to static noise? That's divergence — a real breakdown, not just a bounce.

cards.title: 'The loss isn't going down' doesn't always mean failure
cards.body: With most models, a loss that refuses to trend downward means something's wrong. With GANs, a generator loss that bounces around the same range for a long time — while sample quality visibly improves — can be completely fine. Loss value alone can be a misleading signal for GANs; you often have to look at generated samples too.

cards.title: Oscillating vs. diverging
cards.body: Two curve shapes that look similar at a glance but mean very different things:

left.text:
Bounces within a range
Repeats over many epochs
Samples keep improving

right.text:
Shoots toward 0 or infinity
Doesn't come back down
Samples degrade to noise

exercises.prompt: Which of these loss-curve behaviors is generally considered a real red flag in GAN training, rather than normal oscillation?

options.text: One network's loss shooting sharply toward zero or infinity and never coming back, while output quality collapses
Discriminator loss wobbling gently between 0.5 and 0.8 over many epochs while sample quality keeps improving
Losses bouncing within a stable range for hundreds of epochs
Loss values that never form a single perfectly flat line

exercises.explain: A one-directional runaway toward 0 or infinity, paired with collapsing output quality, is divergence — the actual failure mode, not ordinary bouncing.

exercises.prompt: True or false: In GAN training, a loss curve that never flattens into a smooth, stable line always means training has failed.
exercises.explain: False — GAN loss curves often oscillate throughout training even when the generator is genuinely producing better results. A non-flat curve isn't automatically failure.

exercises.sentence: Because loss values alone can be misleading in GAN training, practitioners often check the generator's actual output ___ alongside the loss curves.
exercises.explain: Since loss numbers can look similar for 'healthy oscillation' and 'quietly stuck,' checking actual generated samples gives you the fuller picture.

exercises.prompt: Which pattern in a GAN's training logs is the clearest sign of healthy, normal oscillation rather than a broken run?

options.text: Discriminator accuracy cycling between roughly 55% and 80% over hundreds of epochs, with generated images gradually looking sharper and more realistic
Discriminator accuracy locking at exactly 100% by epoch 5 and staying there for the rest of training
Generator loss climbing without bound while every generated image turns into solid gray noise

exercises.explain: Steady cycling within a reasonable range, paired with visibly improving outputs, is the signature of healthy oscillation.

exercises.prompt: You now know that oscillating loss curves aren't automatically a failure sign. Given that, what's the smartest way to judge whether a GAN's training run is actually going well?

options.text: Look at both the loss curves' general behavior AND the actual generated samples over time, not the loss numbers alone
Only ever look at whether the loss reaches exactly zero
Stop training the instant any bounce appears in the loss curve
Ignore the loss curves entirely and only count total training time

exercises.explain: Exactly — combine curve behavior with a look at real generated samples, since loss numbers alone can be ambiguous for GANs. Next: some concrete tricks for keeping that oscillation under control.
